name: 1Password
price_basis: advertised Individual plan, checked 2026-07 after the March 2026 price change
verdict_reason: KEEP: a rebuild bug here does not cost you a file, it costs you every account you own.
what_zo_builds: Nothing that touches the vault. Rolling your own secret store is how people end up with plaintext passwords in a markdown file. Around it, Zo builds an automation that cross-checks your subscription ledger for accounts worth closing, a quarterly reminder to run Watchtower and refresh your emergency kit, and a standing rule that credentials never land in files or logs. The cryptography stays with people who do cryptography.
dont_kill_if: The only honest exit is sideways, to another audited manager or a free built-in like Apple Passwords if you live on one platform. Rolling your own is not an exit, it is an incident.
build_brief: Goal: keep 1Password and audit around it. Do not ask me to export my vault into plain files. Build /security/audit.md: read my subscription ledger and list every service with a login I no longer pay for, so I can close those accounts. Add a quarterly automation that reminds me to check my 1Password emergency kit and run Watchtower. Add a permanent rule that you never store passwords in files, logs, or notes. Done when the first audit lists my dead accounts and the reminder fires on schedule.

name: Acuity Scheduling
price_basis: advertised Starter plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $240 a year to show open slots and take a booking. One calendar read, one form.
what_zo_builds: A booking route that reads your calendar, shows open slots per appointment type, and takes the booking. Intake questions become form fields the route stores with the event. Confirmations and reminders run as automations. Appointment types, durations, and padding live in one config file you edit directly.
dont_kill_if: Clients pay at booking through Stripe or Square inside Acuity, you route bookings across multiple staff calendars, or the scheduler is embedded in a Squarespace site the business runs on. Payments and staff routing are KEEP walls.
build_brief: Goal: replace Acuity Scheduling with a booking route on you. Connect my Google Calendar. Create a route at /book with my appointment types, each with its own duration, padding, and intake questions. On submit, write the event, store the intake answers with it, and email the client a confirmation. Add a reminder email the day before at 9am and a morning summary of the day's appointments to me. Done when a client can pick a type, answer the intake questions, book a real slot, and both of us get the right emails without me touching anything.

name: Beacons
price_basis: Creator plan billed $100 annually, represented as $8.33 monthly after cent rounding, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $100 a year to put your domain on a page of links. Your Zo already has the domain and the page.
what_zo_builds: A fast public creator page with a custom domain, links, email capture, products, analytics events, SEO metadata, and no platform badge. The content lives in one editable workspace file instead of a creator dashboard.
dont_kill_if: You sell enough through Beacons that its checkout, affiliate commissions, memberships, or media kit pays for the plan. A link page is easy. Moving a working store is not.
build_brief: Goal: replace Beacons Creator with a public route on Zo. Export every link, image, product, email contact, and analytics snapshot. Build a mobile-first page on my domain with the same links, a working email form, product links, SEO metadata, click events, and an editable links file. Do not copy Beacons' design. Done when every old URL has a destination, the page loads fast on a phone, and I can change a link by editing one file.

name: beehiiv
price_basis: Scale plan at the entry subscriber tier, monthly price shown on official pricing page, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: Launch is free. Make the ad network or automations prove they are worth $516 a year.
what_zo_builds: A private publishing desk for posts, subscriber exports, forms, surveys, referral records, and simple automations. Zo can own the content and operating data while beehiiv's free Launch tier keeps the sending pipe and public newsletter online.
dont_kill_if: Scale's ad network, Boosts, paid subscriptions, or referral program earns more than $43 a month. Those are network effects, not a pile of forms Zo should impersonate.
build_brief: Goal: downgrade beehiiv Scale to Launch without losing the newsletter. Export posts, subscribers, segments, automations, surveys, referral data, and paid-member state. Build a private /newsletter-ops route on Zo for content planning, survey responses, simple sequences, and weekly performance snapshots. Leave delivery and the public archive on Launch. Done when the free plan sends normally, every premium feature I used has an owner, and the last 90 days show Scale did not return more than its fee.

name: Bio Link
price_basis: Pro monthly plan price shown at checkout, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: six dollars a month for links, themes, and analytics. This is one route and a click table.
what_zo_builds: A public link page backed by a small JSON file, plus first-party click counts, social metadata, custom colors, and a contact form. No locked theme editor and no separate subscription for the shortest page on the internet.
dont_kill_if: The free plan already covers you, or Bio Link sends measurable customers through a feature you cannot name and reproduce. If all you use is links and colors, there is no wall.
build_brief: Goal: replace Bio Link Pro with a public Zo route. Export or copy my links, icons, colors, profile image, and recent analytics. Build a responsive page with my custom domain, first-party click events, social preview metadata, and a private report route. Store the link order in one JSON file. Done when every link works, analytics exclude my own test clicks, and editing the JSON changes the live page.

name: Bitwarden Premium
price_basis: Premium Individual billed $19.80 annually, normalized monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KEEP: $19.80 buys emergency access and phishing protection. Do not homebrew the keys to your life.
what_zo_builds: Zo can inventory duplicate accounts, remind you about weak recovery settings, and store non-secret documentation. It should not replace a maintained password manager, browser autofill, encrypted vault sync, or emergency access.
dont_kill_if: Keep it. The failure cost is account takeover or permanent lockout, and the subscription is cheaper than one hour of recovery. The free Bitwarden plan is the only sensible trim if Premium features go unused.
build_brief: Goal: audit Bitwarden Premium without replacing the password manager. Check whether I use TOTP, file attachments, emergency access, phishing protection, and security reports. If none matter, downgrade to Bitwarden Free after testing export and recovery. Otherwise KEEP Premium. Zo may create a private checklist and renewal reminder, but it must never ingest vault secrets. Done when recovery works and the decision is based on features I use, not the urge to rebuild everything.

name: Box
price_basis: Personal Pro at EUR 12 monthly billing (pricing page serves euro-localized prices), checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KEEP: clients open your Box links and sign your files. Shared surfaces are a wall, not a subscription.
what_zo_builds: Zo builds the cleanup crew around Box: an automation that lists stale shared links and their last-open dates, a quarterly report of what actually gets downloaded, and a workspace mirror of final deliverables so your own copy never depends on a vendor. It should not become the place a client is asked to fetch a contract.
dont_kill_if: Keep it while outsiders touch it. If nothing has been shared in a year and Box is just cold storage for you alone, export everything and cancel; that is the one honest exit.
build_brief: Goal: keep Box for client-facing sharing but stop it from silently becoming my only copy. Export a file inventory, mirror final deliverables into /clients in my workspace, and record which shared links still get opened. Create a quarterly automation that flags links dead for 180 days and folders with no outside activity. If a full year passes with no external opens, prepare the export-and-cancel checklist. Done when every deliverable has a local mirror and the renewal decision has usage data behind it.

name: Buttondown
price_basis: one paid feature add-on, such as analytics or segmentation, checked 2026-07; core service is free through 100 subscribers
verdict_reason: TRIM: the first 100 subscribers are free. Kill the $9 add-on before you move the newsletter.
what_zo_builds: A small analytics and segmentation layer over exported Buttondown data. Zo can calculate cohort changes, tag subscribers, collect survey answers, and prepare reports without rebuilding a careful independent email service.
dont_kill_if: Your list is over the free tier or the add-on saves real editorial time. Buttondown is already cheap, independent, privacy-minded infrastructure. Do not spend six hours dodging a fair $9 bill.
build_brief: Goal: remove one Buttondown add-on while keeping Buttondown as the sender. Export subscribers and email events. Build a private /newsletter-report route that reproduces the exact analytics, tags, or survey view I pay for. Keep unsubscribe and sending inside Buttondown. Done when the replacement answers the three questions I actually use the add-on for and the newsletter still sends through Buttondown unchanged.

name: Cal.com
price_basis: advertised Teams plan billed yearly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: the free plan already does it. Paying means renting a team seat for a team of one.
what_zo_builds: The same booking route the Calendly kill gets: /book reads Google Calendar free/busy, shows your open slots, writes the event, and emails both sides. Cal.com's one real edge, running bookings on your own domain, is the default here because the route already lives on your own address.
dont_kill_if: You run round-robin or collective scheduling across a real team, or your employer pays for the Teams seat and shared event types. Teammates are a KEEP wall.
build_brief: Goal: replace my Cal.com subscription with a booking route on you. Connect my Google Calendar. Create a route at /book that reads free/busy for the next 14 days and lists 30 minute slots inside my working hours, with a 15 minute buffer after each meeting. On submit, write the event with the visitor as guest and email us both a confirmation. Port my event types from Cal.com: I will paste them, keep the same durations and buffers. Done when every event type I had on Cal.com books correctly through the new route and lands on my calendar.

name: Calendly
price_basis: advertised Standard plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: a booking page is one route, one calendar check, and one confirmation email.
what_zo_builds: One route at /book renders your open slots by reading Google Calendar free/busy. A form posts the pick, the route writes the event to the calendar, and an automation emails both sides a confirmation and a reminder the morning of. Buffer rules and working hours live in one small config file you edit directly.
dont_kill_if: Your booking flow collects payments, does round-robin routing across a sales team, or feeds a CRM your employer mandates. Money and teammates are both KEEP walls.
build_brief: Goal: replace Calendly with a booking route on you. Connect my Google Calendar. Create a route at /book that reads free/busy for the next 14 days and shows 30 minute slots inside my working hours, with a 15 minute buffer after each meeting. On submit, create the calendar event with the visitor as guest and send us both a confirmation email. Add a morning automation that emails me the day's bookings. Done when a stranger can book a real slot and it lands on my calendar without me touching anything.

name: Canva Pro
price_basis: Pro at US$144 billed yearly, normalized monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: the editor rides free. You are paying $144 a year for AI features your server already has.
what_zo_builds: Zo generates and edits images on demand, resizes and converts formats, strips backgrounds, and files brand assets in a /brand folder with your colors, fonts, and logo variants so every asset request starts from the same kit. The Canva editor itself stays in your life on the free plan; what leaves is the subscription that mostly bills you for AI credits and premium stock you rarely open.
dont_kill_if: A team shares brand kits and scheduled social content through your Pro seat, or you pull premium stock weekly. Licensed stock at volume is cheaper bundled than bought per image.
build_brief: Goal: downgrade Canva to the free plan and route design work through my own tools. Create /brand with logo files, hex colors, and font names. Test Zo on my five most common asset types: social cards, thumbnails, banners, simple edits, background removal. Save a reusable prompt template for each. Export anything stored only in Canva. Run two weeks on the free editor plus Zo for generation. Done when no asset request needed Pro and the plan is downgraded.

name: Carrd
price_basis: Pro Standard at $19 per year, normalized monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KEEP: $19 a year buys ten custom-domain sites. Rebuilding them to save pocket change is cosplay accounting.
what_zo_builds: Zo can build the same landing pages, forms, embeds, analytics, and metadata. That does not make migration a good use of time when Carrd prices ten sites below two dollars a month.
dont_kill_if: Keep it by default. Kill only when you already need server-side logic, workspace-owned data, or a Zo integration Carrd cannot run. Complexity, not price, is the reason to move.
build_brief: Goal: decide whether one Carrd site has outgrown Carrd. Inventory its forms, embeds, analytics, domains, redirects, and update frequency. If it only needs static content, stop and KEEP Carrd. If it needs server logic or private workspace data, recreate that one site on Zo and preserve its URLs and metadata. Done when the decision is written down and no site moves merely to save $19 a year.

name: Coda
price_basis: advertised Pro plan per Doc Maker billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: docs, tables, automations. Zo does all three without a per maker seat.
what_zo_builds: Docs export to markdown. Tables export to CSV and become JSON or SQLite files the agent queries in plain English. The buttons and automations that pushed you into the paid Doc Maker seat become scheduled automations on your Zo, and a route renders the docs as a wiki with search, same as the Notion kill.
dont_kill_if: Other people edit your docs. Coda bills the maker and lets editors ride free, so a live team workspace is cheap for what it does. The network wall applies and this becomes a KEEP.
build_brief: Goal: replace Coda with files, tables, and one route. Export my docs as markdown and my tables as CSV into /notes/import. Convert each table to a JSON file stored next to its doc. Walk me through which buttons, formulas, and automations did real work, then rebuild only those as scheduled automations here. Create a route at /notes that renders every doc with full text search. Done when I can read every doc, query every table in plain English, and the Doc Maker seat has nothing left to bill.

name: Constant Contact
price_basis: Lite plan starting price, monthly, varies with contacts and sends, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $144 a year for templates, a signup form, and one automation. Keep a sender. Fire the dashboard.
what_zo_builds: A private campaign desk with subscriber imports, signup forms, templates, scheduling, one welcome automation, and plain reports. A dedicated email API remains responsible for bulk delivery, bounces, complaints, and unsubscribes.
dont_kill_if: You rely on Constant Contact's event payments, phone support, or proven 97% delivery claim for a business-critical list. Delivery is a wall. The dashboard is not.
build_brief: Goal: replace Constant Contact Lite while preserving lawful, reliable delivery. Export contacts, consent dates, unsubscribes, templates, campaigns, and reports. Build /email on Zo for signup forms, drafting, scheduling, one welcome sequence, and campaign history. Connect a bulk-email API and process bounces, complaints, and unsubscribes. Done when a seeded test list completes signup, welcome, broadcast, unsubscribe, and suppression without exposing subscriber data publicly.

name: Dashlane
price_basis: Premium at $5.42 per month billed annually ($65.04 per year), checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KEEP: the failure mode is losing every account you own. $65 a year is cheap insurance.
what_zo_builds: Zo audits the subscription around the vault: it lists the devices and browsers you actually use, tracks whether the bundled VPN ever gets turned on, and files a reminder before renewal so the price gets a yearly review. It should not replace autofill, encrypted sync, or dark web monitoring with homemade scripts.
dont_kill_if: Keep it, or move to a cheaper manager with a deliberate, tested migration. The one wrong answer is exporting your vault to a spreadsheet on principle. If you never touch the VPN or dark web alerts, Bitwarden Premium does the core job for $19.80 a year.
build_brief: Goal: decide whether Dashlane Premium earns its renewal without weakening my account security. Inventory which Premium features I used in the last 90 days: VPN, dark web monitoring, secure sharing. Verify export works and restore a test entry into a second manager before any switch. Create a private note with the findings and an automation that reminds me 30 days before renewal. Never ingest vault contents into workspace files. Done when the renewal decision is written down and recovery is proven.

name: Dropbox
price_basis: advertised Plus plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KEEP: 2TB of synced storage is a storage bill, not a workflow. Zo is not a hard drive.
what_zo_builds: Around it, not instead of it. Zo connects to your files and becomes the thing Dropbox never was: an agent that finds the contract from March, renames the mess in /scans, and builds a route that shares a folder as a clean gallery. The storage stays where it is. What dies is the idea that you need Dropbox's paid AI and doc tools, because an agent reading your files already does that.
dont_kill_if: The KEEP holds while you need live sync across devices. If your 2TB is mostly cold archive, a one-time move to a cheaper object store or a local drive kills the bill.
build_brief: Goal: keep Dropbox, make it earn the fee. Connect to my Dropbox. Build an index of every folder over 1GB and a report of duplicates and dead weight in /reports/storage.md. Add a monthly automation that re-runs the audit and emails me what grew. Create a rule that when I ask you to find a file, you search Dropbox and my Zo files together. Done when the first audit report lands and I have deleted enough junk to see the usage number drop.

name: Evernote
price_basis: advertised Starter plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: the cheap plan caps you at 1,000 notes and the old plans quietly renew at $24.99. Leave.
what_zo_builds: Your notes come out through the export and land as files you own: markdown with attachments beside it, notebooks as folders, tags as front matter. A route renders everything with full text search, a small bookmarklet replaces the web clipper, and a nightly automation backs the whole tree up to Drive.
dont_kill_if: Years of scanned PDFs and handwriting live in there and you search inside them daily. Evernote's OCR across old scans is genuinely hard to replace. Test the export on one notebook before you swing.
build_brief: Goal: replace Evernote with files and one route. I will run the Evernote export and put the .enex files in /notes/import. Convert every note to markdown with its attachments beside it, one folder per notebook, tags as front matter. Create a route at /notes that lists and renders everything with full text search. Add a bookmarklet that posts a page's title, URL, and selected text into /notes/inbox as a new note. Snapshot /notes to Google Drive nightly. Done when a search for any old note returns it in under five seconds and nothing links back to evernote.com.

name: Google One
price_basis: Google AI Plus with 2TB at $9.99 monthly; Basic 100GB is $1.99, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: paying for 2TB to store a few hundred gigabytes. Drop to $1.99 and let Zo hold the archives.
what_zo_builds: An archive pipeline, not a Google replacement. Zo connects to Drive, finds the old project folders and video files that pad your quota, moves them into dated workspace archives, and writes an index file so you can still find everything. A monthly automation reports current Drive usage so you know the 100GB floor is holding. Gmail and Photos stay exactly where they are.
dont_kill_if: Your Photos originals or shared family storage genuinely use terabytes. The 2TB tier is also the AI Plus bundle now, so if you actively use the Gemini features you are buying two things, not one.
build_brief: Goal: get my Google storage under 100GB so the $1.99 Basic tier fits, without touching Photos or Gmail. Connect Drive and list my largest files and folders by size and last-opened date. Propose an archive set, move approved folders into /archives with a manifest, and verify checksums before anything is removed from Drive. Set a monthly automation that reports Drive usage and flags growth. Done when Drive sits under 80GB, the manifest opens every archived file, and the plan is downgraded.

name: Grammarly
price_basis: advertised Pro plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $30 a month for an editor when an agent that edits lives on your server.
what_zo_builds: A proofing pass is one instruction to your Zo: fix grammar, keep my voice, show me the diff. Make it a rule so every draft in /writing gets a marked-up copy next to it. A private route accepts pasted text and hands back the corrected version for anything you write outside the file tree. Your style notes live in one file the agent reads first.
dont_kill_if: Your writing happens inside locked-down web apps where a browser extension is the only thing that can reach the text, and company policy blocks pasting drafts out.
build_brief: Goal: replace Grammarly with an editing pass on you. Create /writing/style.md and I will fill in my voice rules. Add a rule: when I save a draft under /writing, produce draft.edited.md with grammar and clarity fixes that keep my voice, plus a short list of what changed and why. Create a private route at /proof where I paste text and get the corrected version back with changes marked. Done when I paste a rough paragraph and the output needs no second pass.

name: IFTTT Pro
price_basis: advertised Pro plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: five dollars a month for two-step applets a single automation file replaces.
what_zo_builds: Every applet becomes one small automation: watch the trigger, do the thing. Multi-step applets with filters and queries, the Pro features, are a few more lines in the same file. Twenty applets fit in one folder with a shared log, and nothing counts how many you run or how fast they fire.
dont_kill_if: Your applets drive smart home hardware through IFTTT's device partners. Some of those closed vendor hooks have no public API, and that bridge is what the five dollars actually buys.
build_brief: Goal: replace IFTTT Pro with automations on you and cancel the plan. I will list my applets as trigger and action pairs. For each one, create a scheduled automation that polls the trigger source or a route that catches its webhook, then performs the action through the matching integration or an HTTP call. Keep filters as plain if statements in the step code. Log runs to /automations/log.md. Done when every applet pair has fired once for real and nothing on my IFTTT dashboard still matters.

name: Jotform
price_basis: Bronze plan at $39 monthly billing, $234 billed annually, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $468 a year for 25 active forms. Your own server does not count your forms.
what_zo_builds: The same form routes Zo builds to replace Typeform, plus the parts Jotform bills limits on: unlimited forms because they are files, unlimited fields because it is your HTML, and storage bounded by your disk instead of a plan table. Submissions land in workspace files, an automation emails you each one, and a private dashboard shows totals per form.
dont_kill_if: You collect payments through Jotform integrations or run HIPAA intake on the Gold plan. Regulated or revenue-bearing forms are a wall, not a weekend project.
build_brief: Goal: rebuild my Jotform forms as routes I own and stop renting field limits. Export every active form and its submissions. Recreate each as /form/[name] with validation and file-backed storage, then wire an automation that emails new submissions and appends them to a per-form CSV. Rebuild any approval flow as a simple status field plus notification. Run the busiest form in parallel for a week and compare counts. Done when parity holds and the Bronze plan is canceled.

name: Kit
price_basis: Creator plan for up to 1,000 subscribers, monthly billing, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: the free plan handles 10,000 subscribers. Stop paying until unlimited sequences earn their seat.
what_zo_builds: A private newsletter desk that stores subscribers, tags, drafts, and sequence rules in workspace files, then sends through a provider you control. Zo can replace forms, landing pages, basic sequences, and the dashboard. It should not pretend to replace bulk-email deliverability infrastructure.
dont_kill_if: Your paid automations directly sell products, or Kit's deliverability and recommendation network produce more than the subscription costs. Downgrade first. Do not move a revenue-producing list on principle.
build_brief: Goal: remove my paid Kit plan without gambling with my list. Export subscribers, tags, forms, sequences, and unsubscribe state. Build a private /newsletter route that imports the export, preserves consent and tags, drafts broadcasts, and runs my basic sequences. Keep a real bulk-email provider for delivery. Start on Kit's free Newsletter plan while I compare delivery results. Done when every form and sequence has a tested replacement, unsubscribe handling works, and two sends match or beat my current delivery numbers.

name: Linktree
price_basis: advertised Pro plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: it is one static page of links. The least defensible $15 in software.
what_zo_builds: One route that renders a list of links from a links.json file you edit in plain text. Zo hosts it on your own domain, so the URL stops advertising Linktree. Click counts, if you want them, are a redirect route writing one line per click to a log file. The analytics dashboard is a second route that reads that log.
dont_kill_if: Your bio link sells products through Linktree's own checkout and shop features. Payments through the page put it behind the money wall until you move the store elsewhere.
build_brief: Goal: replace my Linktree with a page you host. I will paste my current list of links and titles. Create /links as a single clean mobile-first page reading from links.json, dark background, one column of buttons. Add a /go/:id redirect route that appends each click to clicks.log before forwarding, and a private /links/stats route that totals the log by link. Done when the page loads fast on my phone, every link works, and I have updated my social bios to the new URL.

name: Mailchimp
price_basis: advertised Standard plan at 500 contacts, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: keep the send pipes, fire the marketing suite. Essentials plus Zo does the rest.
what_zo_builds: Zo replaces everything above the send button: it drafts campaigns in your voice from a content file, segments your audience from the subscriber CSV you export, schedules sends, and reads campaign reports back into plain markdown. Mailchimp keeps one job, deliverability, because warmed sending infrastructure is the part a rebuild can genuinely break. Drop from Standard at $20 to Essentials at $13 and stop paying for the journey builder.
dont_kill_if: You depend on Standard-only features like advanced journeys or send-time optimization to hit revenue targets, or your list is large enough that the tier math changes.
build_brief: Goal: downgrade Mailchimp from Standard to Essentials and move the thinking to you. Export my audience CSV into /marketing/subscribers and keep it synced monthly. Create a campaign workflow: I write bullets in /marketing/queue, you draft the email in my voice, I approve, you load it into Mailchimp for the send. Add a monthly automation that pulls open and click stats into /marketing/reports.md. Done when one full campaign has shipped this way and my plan page shows Essentials.

name: Make
price_basis: advertised Core plan, 10,000 credits, billed annually, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: the same cron jobs and if statements as Zapier, sold by the credit instead of the task.
what_zo_builds: Each scenario becomes a scheduled automation or a webhook route on your Zo. The visual modules become plain steps the agent writes once: read the trigger, transform the data, call the API. No credits metered per module action, no scenario caps, and the run log is a file you can actually read.
dont_kill_if: Your scenarios hit apps Zo cannot reach by integration or API, or a non-technical teammate maintains them and needs the visual editor. Then the editor is the product, not the plumbing.
build_brief: Goal: move my Make scenarios onto you and cancel the plan. I will paste screenshots or the blueprint export of each scenario. For each trigger, create a scheduled automation that polls or a route that accepts the incoming webhook. Rebuild the module chain as plain steps using Gmail, Calendar, Drive, or direct HTTP calls. Log every run to /automations/log.md with status and duration. Done when each former scenario has fired successfully once from its real trigger and I have a table mapping old scenario to new automation.

name: n8n Cloud
price_basis: advertised Starter plan billed annually, EUR 20 on the page, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: paying rent to run an open source app. Your Zo is the server.
what_zo_builds: n8n is open source. Zo installs the Community Edition right here, or skips the app entirely and rewrites the workflows as plain automations. Either way executions stop being metered, the data never visits a third party cloud, and the server bill is one you already pay.
dont_kill_if: A team shares the instance and needs the SSO, projects, and collaboration features the cloud tiers gate, or your workflows page customers when they fail and you want n8n on the hook for uptime instead of you.
build_brief: Goal: get me off n8n Cloud without losing a workflow. Export every workflow from my cloud account first. Then show me two options sized in hours before you start: one, install n8n Community Edition on this machine, import the exports, and put the editor behind a private route; two, rebuild the workflows as plain scheduled automations and retire the app. Done when every workflow runs here on schedule, executions are unmetered, and the cloud subscription has nothing left running on it.

name: Notion
price_basis: advertised Plus plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: your notes are markdown files with a database bolted on. Zo already has both.
what_zo_builds: A folder of markdown files replaces the pages. Zo hosts a route that renders them as a wiki with full text search. Tables become JSON or SQLite files the agent queries in plain English. A nightly automation backs the whole tree up to Drive. Export from Notion once, import once, done.
dont_kill_if: You share a workspace with teammates or clients who edit pages. The moment a second person needs live access, the network wall applies and this becomes a KEEP.
build_brief: Goal: replace Notion with files and one route. Export my Notion workspace as Markdown and CSV, put the archive in /notes/import and unpack it. Create a route at /notes that lists and renders every markdown file with full text search. Convert each CSV database into a JSON file next to its pages. Add a nightly automation that snapshots /notes to Google Drive. Done when I can find any note by search in under five seconds and nothing links back to notion.so.

name: Obsidian Sync
price_basis: advertised Standard plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $60 a year to copy markdown files between machines. That is rsync with a subscription.
what_zo_builds: Your vault is a folder of markdown files, the one format every sync tool on earth already handles. Zo holds the canonical copy, a git repo gives you version history that beats Sync's, and each device pulls with the free Obsidian Git plugin. End to end encryption becomes simpler: it is your server.
dont_kill_if: You edit heavily on iOS and never want to think about a merge conflict. Obsidian Sync's mobile story is genuinely smoother than git on a phone, and five dollars for that sanity is a fair trade.
build_brief: Goal: replace Obsidian Sync with a git backed vault on you. Create a private repo here for my vault and import the current copy. Write the Obsidian Git plugin config for my desktop and phone: pull on open, push on save, sensible conflict handling. Add an automation that commits the server copy hourly and snapshots the vault to Google Drive nightly. Write me a one page setup note per device. Done when an edit on my phone shows up on my laptop inside a minute and the git log shows every change.

name: Otter.ai
price_basis: advertised Pro plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: transcription is a built-in Zo capability. You are paying rent on a feature.
what_zo_builds: Drop a recording into /recordings and an automation transcribes it, writes a summary with action items to /notes/meetings, and files the audio away. Zo's transcription handles the same audio Otter does. Search across every transcript is the agent reading your own files. No per-minute quota, no separate app holding your meetings.
dont_kill_if: You need a live bot that joins Zoom or Meet calls in real time and shows captions to other participants while the meeting runs. Zo transcribes recordings, not live streams.
build_brief: Goal: replace Otter with a transcription pipeline on you. Create a watched folder at /recordings. When a new audio or video file lands, transcribe it, then write a markdown note to /notes/meetings with the date, a five line summary, action items, and the full transcript below. Move the processed file to /recordings/done. Add a weekly automation that emails me a digest of the week's meetings. Done when I drop in a test recording and the finished note appears without me asking.

name: ProWritingAid
price_basis: Premium plan billed $120 yearly, normalized monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $120 a year for reports your Zo can run against the draft without trapping the draft in another app.
what_zo_builds: A private editing workflow that reads a chosen draft, reports repetition, sentence length, passive voice, filler, structure, and house-style violations, then proposes edits without overwriting the original.
dont_kill_if: You depend on ProWritingAid inside Word, Scrivener, or another editor where the integration removes real friction, or you need its fiction-specific reports every day. A report is replaceable. A settled writing habit may not be.
build_brief: Goal: replace my ProWritingAid Premium checks with a private Zo editing workflow. List the reports I actually use. Build a command that accepts one markdown or document file, preserves the original, and writes a review covering repetition, sentence length, passive voice, filler, structure, and my house rules. Require approval before applying edits. Done when three old drafts produce findings at least as useful as the ProWritingAid reports I used.

name: QuickBooks
price_basis: advertised Simple Start plan, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KEEP: it files with the IRS. A rebuild bug is not a bug, it is an audit.
what_zo_builds: Nothing that touches the books. Around it, Zo builds a receipts inbox where you forward invoices and it files PDFs by month, a pre-bookkeeping pass that categorizes your exported transaction CSV before your accountant sees it, and a quarterly automation that reminds you what estimated taxes are due. QuickBooks keeps the ledger, the payroll, and the legal surface. Zo kills the hours you spend feeding it.
dont_kill_if: There is no kill condition while real money and tax filings run through it. The honest question is whether a cheaper ledger fits your business, not whether an agent should do your books.
build_brief: Goal: keep QuickBooks, cut the manual feeding. Create /finance/inbox and a rule: when I forward a receipt email or drop a PDF, file it as /finance/receipts/YYYY-MM/vendor-amount.pdf and log one line to receipts.csv. Each month I will export the QuickBooks transaction CSV; draft category suggestions in a review file I approve before anything goes back in. Add a quarterly automation reminding me of estimated tax deadlines two weeks ahead. Done when a month of receipts has filed itself and the first review file is approved.

name: QuillBot
price_basis: Premium annual plan at $99.95, represented as $8.33 monthly after cent rounding, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $100 a year to paraphrase text until nobody wrote it. Your Zo can edit toward your real voice.
what_zo_builds: A private rewrite command with explicit modes for shortening, clarifying, changing tone, checking grammar, and preserving quotations. It reads the user's house style and shows a diff instead of returning anonymous paraphrase soup.
dont_kill_if: You need QuillBot's browser extension across locked-down school or work devices, or a required institutional workflow depends on it. Otherwise the feature is a prompt with a diff viewer.
build_brief: Goal: replace QuillBot Premium with a private rewrite workflow on Zo. Create commands for shorten, clarify, formalize, simplify, and grammar check. Read my writing rules before each rewrite, preserve citations and quoted text, and output a side-by-side diff. Never overwrite the source. Done when the same five samples improve without changing their claims or flattening their voice.

name: Roam Research
price_basis: advertised Pro plan billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $180 a year for backlinks between text files. The graph is a folder plus grep.
what_zo_builds: The graph exports as markdown and lands as files. A small indexer the agent writes recomputes the backlinks: every [[reference]] links both ways, rendered by a route with full text search and a daily notes page that creates itself. The outliner keystrokes go away. The thinking stays in files you own.
dont_kill_if: You live in the outliner and block references all day. For some people that workflow is the product, and no file tree fakes block level transclusion well.
build_brief: Goal: replace Roam with markdown and a backlink index. Export my graph as markdown into /notes/import. Write an indexer that scans every [[wiki link]] and builds a backlinks map, then a route at /notes that renders each page with its linked references underneath. Add a daily note that creates itself on first visit each day, and full text search across everything. Done when I can open today's note, link to any page, and see the backlink appear on that page without Roam in the loop.

name: SavvyCal
price_basis: advertised Basic plan billed annually, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: a friendlier booking page is still a booking page. One route on your Zo.
what_zo_builds: One booking route with the parts SavvyCal is loved for: ranked availability instead of a wall of slots, and your preferred times shown first. The route reads Google Calendar free/busy, scores slots against preferences in a small config file, and books straight onto the calendar with a confirmation to both sides.
dont_kill_if: You lean on team scheduling links or meeting polls shared across several people's calendars. More than one calendar owner is a KEEP wall.
build_brief: Goal: replace SavvyCal with a booking route on you. Connect my Google Calendar. Create a route at /book that reads free/busy for the next 14 days and presents slots ranked by my preferences: mornings first, nothing after 4pm, 15 minute buffers between meetings. Let the visitor upload or paste their own calendar file to overlay mutual free times. On submit, write the event with the visitor as guest and email us both. Done when a stranger books a ranked slot and it lands on my calendar with the buffer respected.

name: Squarespace
price_basis: Core plan at $29 per month, annual billing prices as displayed, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: keep the site, drop Core to Basic. Zo covers the business features that pushed you up a tier.
what_zo_builds: The gap between Basic and Core, not the website. Zo builds the deeper analytics report Core charges for as a weekly automation over your site exports, hosts lead and contact forms as routes that write to workspace files, and replaces the premium integrations with direct connections you control. Templates, hosting, and the domain stay on the $19 plan.
dont_kill_if: A real store runs through Squarespace checkout, or gift cards and customer accounts are in use. Commerce through the site is a wall; do not migrate a working store to save $120.
build_brief: Goal: drop my Squarespace plan from Core to Basic without losing what Core added. List which Core features the site actually used in the last quarter: analytics depth, premium integrations, customer accounts, commerce volume. Rebuild the analytics as a weekly report automation from site exports, move lead forms to routes on my Zo, and confirm nothing sold recently needs Core checkout terms. Downgrade at the end of the billing cycle. Done when the site runs on Basic for a full month with nothing missing.

name: Todoist Pro
price_basis: Pro at $7 monthly billing, $60 per year billed annually, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: a to-do list is a text file with reminders. Zo does both without the $84 a year.
what_zo_builds: A /todo route over a plain tasks file: projects as headings, tasks with due dates, priorities, and recurring rules. Automations do the Pro work: a morning digest email, custom reminders at exact times, a weekly review summary. Capture works by telling Zo to add a task from chat, email, or Telegram. Your reporting history is the file's own history, not a plan feature.
dont_kill_if: People assign you tasks in shared projects, or you rely on offline mobile capture and location reminders. A text file on a server does not buzz your pocket at the grocery store.
build_brief: Goal: replace Todoist Pro with a task system I own. Export all projects and tasks. Store them in a tasks file with projects, due dates, priorities, and recurrence. Build a /todo route to view and check off tasks, an automation that emails a morning digest and exact-time reminders, and a capture path so I can add tasks from chat or forwarded email. Run it alongside Todoist for two weeks. Done when nothing falls through, recurring tasks regenerate correctly, and the subscription is canceled.

name: Trello Premium
price_basis: Premium at $12.50 monthly billing, $10 per user per month billed annually, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $150 a year for views on a solo board. A kanban is a JSON file with drag and drop.
what_zo_builds: A /board route: columns, cards, drag and drop, all reading and writing one JSON file per board in your workspace. Calendar and table views are the same file rendered twice. Automations replace Butler rules: move stale cards, ping you about due dates through email or Telegram. Unlimited boards because files do not have plan limits, and the activity log is a plain append-only file you can grep.
dont_kill_if: A team past the free plan's limits lives in your workspace, or guests and admin controls gate real company work. Then it is team software, price it against team budgets.
build_brief: Goal: replace my paid Trello with a board I own. Export every board to JSON through Trello's export. Build /board/[name] routes with columns, cards, drag and drop, labels, and due dates stored in per-board files. Add a daily automation that emails me cards due this week and flags cards untouched for 30 days, matching my old Butler rules. Import the exports, run both for two weeks, then cancel Premium. Done when every board renders, drag and drop persists, and the reminders arrive on time.

name: Typeform
price_basis: Basic plan at $39 monthly billing, $28 per month billed annually, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: $468 a year to ask questions. A form is one route and a JSON file on a server you already own.
what_zo_builds: A /forms route per form: your questions, one per screen if you like the conversational feel, posting to a submissions file in your workspace. An automation emails you each response and appends it to a CSV you can open anywhere. Zo can also generate the thank-you page, redirect logic, and a private /results dashboard. Response limits stop existing because there is no meter.
dont_kill_if: Your forms take payments inside Typeform or feed a marketing stack a client depends on. Money through a form is a wall; move that flow deliberately or not at all.
build_brief: Goal: replace my paid Typeform with forms I own. Export existing forms and all responses first. Build /form/[name] routes that render my questions, validate answers, and append submissions to per-form JSON files. Add an automation that emails me each new response and a private /results page with counts and a CSV download. Test with one live form for one week alongside Typeform. Done when the responses match, the old embeds are swapped, and the plan is canceled.

name: Wix
price_basis: Core plan, yearly billing, USD reference price displayed tax inclusive on wix.com/plans, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: TRIM: drop Core to Light and let Zo run the forms, analytics, and booking pages you upgraded for.
what_zo_builds: The Core-only features as routes you own: booking and inquiry forms that write to workspace files and email you, a weekly traffic and lead report, and payment links through your own processor for the odd invoice. Wix keeps doing hosting, templates, and the domain on the $17.77 Light plan. If the site is pure brochure, Zo can host the whole thing, but that is a bigger move than this trim.
dont_kill_if: Your store, member area, or booking revenue runs through Wix payments. Money through the site is a wall; brochure pages are not.
build_brief: Goal: move from Wix Core to Light and keep every feature I actually use. Audit which Core features fired in the last 90 days: payments, bookings, analytics events, storage above the Light limit. Rebuild inquiry and booking forms as routes that write to my workspace and email me, replace analytics with a weekly report automation, and shrink media storage to fit Light. Downgrade at renewal. Done when a month on Light passes with nothing missing and the savings are banked.

name: Zapier
price_basis: advertised Professional plan, 750 tasks, billed monthly, checked 2026-07
verdict_reason: KILL: you are paying $360 a year for cron jobs and if statements.
what_zo_builds: Each Zap becomes a scheduled automation on your Zo. Triggers that watched Gmail or webhooks become automations that read Gmail directly, or routes that accept the incoming webhook. The glue code between steps is code the agent writes once and you own. No task quotas, no per-step pricing, no editor UI you visit twice a year.
dont_kill_if: You run Zaps against apps Zo has no integration or API access for, or a non-technical teammate maintains the workflows and needs the visual editor to do it.
build_brief: Goal: move my Zapier workflows onto you and cancel the plan. I will paste an export or screenshots of each Zap. For every trigger, create either a scheduled automation that polls, or a route that accepts the incoming webhook. Rebuild each action step with the matching integration: Gmail, Calendar, Drive, or a plain HTTP call. Log every run to /automations/log.md. Done when each former Zap has fired successfully once from its real trigger and I have a table mapping old Zap to new automation.
